comments.title: A Couple Spagyric Questions
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I intend on making a spagyric tincture of spruce buds this spring - do you happen to know what planet the spruce tree (blue spruce, to be precise) corresponds to?

Also, on the subject of spagyrics, the first one I made (basil) didn’t turn out so great but I’ve held on to it. I’ve heard that people will sometimes use spagyrics as fertilizer for plants/soil. Is this the case and if so can you provide any suggestions on doing so?

Thanks!
-Colter

replies.title: Re: A Couple Spagyric Questions
replies.content:
1) I'm not familiar with the planetary correspondence of spruce; I would guess that it's Saturn, as many conifers are, but don't quote me on that.

2) Yes, you can do that, but it has to be very dilute so the alcohol doesn't harm the plants. Put a tablespoon or so into a watering can and use that to water your garden.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Greetings, JMG.

Today I have two questions regarding e-books your wrote; two problems came to my mind. These questions might benefit your readers, because possibly such thoughts crossed their minds.


1) The first one is practical. For starters, no one is going to give away copies of your books, because of your workings to make them useless if used as pirated copies. So, I paid for an e-book; "it belongs to me now." You have said before that your e-books can be printed, without triggering your protection against piracy.

However, most e-books are sold with protections that make this impossible. These are, as you know, circumventable. The same way I won't surrender my paper books to the censors if possible, I don't want to lose the e-books I "bought" because they were remotely cancelled. So, storing local and unlocked copies is mandatory. This brings the first question.

The first question is, "If I remove DRM from e-books authored by you that I paid for, and I intend to store them for my own use, will this trigger your protection works on them?"

If yes, I need to know this, because if so then your e-books with DRM are useless, and I won't ever buy one again. There are always the paper copies.


2) The second one is sort of ethical. Using advertisement blockers is, in a sense, stealing. Authors putting ads that generate revenue get less of it with the ads not shown. Disclosure, this won't bar me from using adblockers; I'm a very concerned thief. Digital cooties can infect your computer and steal your digital everything, and not using adblockers is unwise. I'm willing to put up with the karma of stealing advertisement revenue from content creators by blocking advertisements, so there goes the problem with ads; the price will be paid, one way or another.

However, your books remain. Making local copies will violate the distribution agreement between the e-book provider and me. In magic, violation of contracts is serious (as in, there will be a price). I am willing to pay the price to remove DRM from my books, but this leads to the second question.

The second question is, "Removing DRM from e-books violates the contract between me and the distributor. Will this violation trigger the workings you made to protect your books?"

Again, if this occurs, "I need to know this, because if so then your e-books with DRM are useless, and I won't ever buy one again. There are always the paper copies."

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I cannot encourage you to remove the DRM from e-books, for obvious legal reasons, but I will say that my working does not make any reference to DRM or other software. It focuses entirely on whether you paid for the book -- new or used -- or got it from a library or in some other legitimate way. Thus, for example, it will not be triggered if you tear out the copyright page or put a different cover on your copy, so long as you did legitimately buy your copy.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: By the way, I also use adblockers. If a site won't let me enter with an adblocker on, I leave the site.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Is paying for the book enough?
Very often ebooks have a cheaper prince in a foreign site, but these are intended to be sold in their own countries. The price is different because of distribution rights. Buying from other countries is in principle illegal, but sellers just ignore it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Er, if you read what I wrote you will find that I've already answered your question.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
My apologies, I thought it would be like the DRM, but it is preferable to err on the side of the caution.

Thanks for writing the books!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Adblocker use is not any form of stealing in any sense, and if you've been lead to believe that it is so your banishing rituals should be directed to the literal malign magics used by the literal evil wizards of digital advertising.

comments.title: Central European Tradition
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

I've seen you describe Franz Bardon's works as being part of the "central European tradition." What are some other books and authors in this tradition? I haven't found anything comparable to Bardon's works, so I'm curious what else is out there.

replies.title: Re: Central European Tradition
replies.content: There's very little else from that tradition in English. Georg Lomer is one of the few exceptions; his Seven Hermetic Letters is worth studying if you like Bardon. Anton Joseph Kirchweger's Aurea Catena Homeri is in a much older version of the same general current. Since my reading knowledge of German is not very good and my reading knowledge of the western Slavic languages is effectively nonexistent, I don't have any other titles or authors to recommend.

replies.title: Re: Central European Tradition
replies.content:
There are a bunch of Czech Occultists in Barron’s tradition releasing translations in English. http://czechhermetics.com/

I haven’t read any of their stuff, but just to let anyone who’s interested know.

replies.title: Re: Central European Tradition
replies.content: Huzzah! This is excellent news. I've bookmarked the website for serious exploration, but at first glance it looks very, very good.

replies.title: Re: Central European Tradition
replies.content: Hey, thanks for the link! That looks like a lot of great information.

replies.title: Re: Central European Tradition
replies.content:
Thanks for the suggestions!

In this post: https://www.ecosophia.net/april-2018-book-club/ you responded to a commenter that you likely wouldn't recommend Bardon's works as a stand alone system.

How would you recommend someone approach them, or incorporate them into other practices for a more well-rounded or balanced system?

replies.title: Re: Central European Tradition
replies.content:
If you're going to take up Bardon's work, start by studying Lévi's Doctrine and Ritual of High Magic, which influenced him powerfully. Then proceed with the lessons in Initiation into Hermetics step by step, completing all three phases of each step before going onto the next one. The new publications coming out may well be worth adding in -- we'll see what comes out.

I tend to recommend other systems instead, but your mileage may vary, and if Bardon's work appeals to you, by all means go for it.

replies.title: Re: Central European Tradition
replies.content:
Awesome, thanks for the suggestions! I have been practicing traditional GD magic from your books, first completing LRM several years ago. I had planned to start with Initiation into Hermetics because it really called to me at the time, but I was a little overwhelmed, and some of the exercises in LRM seemed like a good preparation. I then got sucked into continued GD practice, which I've learned a lot from, but the more I practice, the more the principles I've learned seem best applied by myself not as much in the ritual methods, but in ways that echo Bardon's visualization, meditative, and contemplation-based techniques. I know you recommend mastering one system completely first, but I'm at the point where I'm questioning if the path I'm on is what I want to continue as my only method for the next several years. I'm finding myself increasingly drawn back to Bardon. I may continue the ritual magic a few times a week and begin the practices in IIH, but lots of contemplation and divination is in order to make sure that's best for me.

I know Mark Stavish laid out a study plan combining GD work with IIH here: https://hermetic.com/stavish/outlines/qabala-study, and I do see ways in which the paths complement each other.

replies.title: Re: Central European Tradition
replies.content:
If you're interested in the Bardon Tradition, I've heard glowing and adoring praise laid on the works of Friedrich Wilhelm Quintscher and Emil Stejnar, apparently both are considered genuine successors to the Bardon Tradition who expanded and improved it. Stejnar was apparently the one who realized the beings listed in Bardon's Practice of Magical Evocation were not completely new, but the names of conventional angels and celestial beings encoded with a simple cipher. Unfortunately most of their books are available only in German. A couple of Stejnar's books have been translated to English and his website (https://www.stejnar-verlag.com/home-e.html) says that more translations are forthcoming. On that note, Bardon's son has given the authorization to begin a new translation and publishing of his father's original manuscript a couple years ago, so the decade ahead looks bright for prospective Bardonians. I also have a book published by "The Franz Bardon Community" called Equipoise: Insights into Foundational Astral Training which is a series of essays by various Bardon practitioners. There are also two books by Nenad Djordjevic-Talerman called The Earth Zone and The Moon Zone which also deal with advanced Bardon practice, though I haven't read them so I can't vouch for the quality. The writings of Martin Faulks and William Mistele are also Bardonian focused, though I've never particularly cared one way or the other about the former and the latter seems a bit too new agey.

If you do follow the Bardon path, don't hesitate to post your experiences here. I wanted to take the Bardon path myself but Geomancy pointed me to The Celtic Golden Dawn and it turned out to be exactly what I needed at this point in life.

replies.title: Re: Central European Tradition
replies.content:
Hey, thanks for the links and suggestions!

I was initially drawn to Bardon's works at the beginning of my path, but LRM and some other books drew me aside and I devoted several years to GD magic. I think that too was what I needed at the time, but I'm feeling drawn back to Bardon of late. I frequent JMGs blogs regularly, so I'll be sure to check in if I pursue that further.

comments.title: Results of scrying
comments.content:
Hello, Mr Greer.

I would like to share my advancements with this divination technique. It's been pretty irregular. Most of the times I get no stable images, just noise and too much movement. But sometimes I get fixed images, or slow enough to make sense of them, and even a few have been pretty real, not just shades of lights, but like watching a color TV screen. On the occasion, I have forgotten them before I could put anything in the diary, as if they were dreams.

There's a theme that has repeated a couple of times and I have meditated on it. In those sessions I have seen:
1. A plant.
2. Something related to water.
3. Something related to fire (always a weak form).
4. Something related to wind (always strong and clear).
5. Something related to earth.
6. A green winged dragon who watches me.

Plants and elements are always different images, but the dragon is the same. So I have reflected on him. He is not threatening, he only watches me, the same way I am watching the scrying images, without expectations, without judgement. I feel his confidence and power, he bears no fire and I haven't seen the front legs so far, so he could be a wyvern, but I am still unsure about this. By the looks he can fly, so it could be a wind dragon as well.
After meditating on the images, I have the impression that this dragon is partly me, like a mirror reflection, partly an independent will. Sometimes I don't see the whole dragon, only one eye, but I know it's him. I think it might be my totem. And also that I am weak with fire, whatever that might means.

Now I don't have any idea what to do with this. I didn't expect to have a totem, honestly. Do I have to wear a sigil or anything? I'd rather not have anything for showing, since I want to keep it secret.

replies.title: Re: Results of scrying
replies.content: Don't worry about doing something with it, other than meditating on the imagery and seeing what it has to teach you.

replies.title: Re: Results of scrying
replies.content: Thank you. Will do.

comments.title: Reliving memories
comments.content:
Hello JMG. An occult question will emerge at the end of this long comment. I’ve made it as short as I can.

JMG, some of your recommended reading has been helpful to me. I have in mind Scripts People Live by Claude Steiner, novels by Dione Fortune, The Tacit Dimension by Michale Polanyi, and Dorothy Brande’s Wake Up Live.

First off, I’ve long known that I’ve been emotionally and socially handicapped by some severely dysfunctional upbringing by my parents, with effects to my own happiness I’ll leave you to imagine. I endorse having Asperger’s; this is a label that more or less accurately connotes how others perceive or experience me, but it hasn’t otherwise proved useful. At one point 20 years ago, I was getting trained in social pragmatics, and it soon became clear to me that my model of mind was not the real problem. I sensed an inner saboteur, and that this problem reflected not some sort of deficit, but an active process. I had counseling off and on over 35 years with nothing to show for it. My first big clue came when someone asked you, JMG, maybe 10 years ago, what book influenced you the most, and you replied “The Tacit Dimension” (I’m working from memory here). I immediately read it, and it opened up a whole new aspect of mind which I had hitherto not considered deeply. I was convinced that was where I needed to look to make any progress, and so began a long process that has continued to this day. My dream-life has been an important realm where my progress manifests: over the last several years, a sequence of dreams slowly revealed that “inner saboteur” I had intuited, and hinted a relationship with my early upbringing. So I felt confident I was on the right track with tacit memory. But as certain as I was of my parents’ role in my life, I couldn’t see how their influence was affecting me NOW. A big break came when I discovered shame and shame-based upbringing. That was a bullseye. It was how I was brought up, how I brought up my daughters, and I put a stop to that immediately. I was too late to prevent harming my older daughter, but she has at least has acknowledged the change. Important as that insight was, it was nowhere near sufficient. I was bummed, because for a little while, I thought I was home free.

In my uncomfortable re-exploration of Christianity, I synergistically came across Dione Fortune’s novel ‘The Goat Foot God’, and was fairly smacked by it, as it precipitated insight into how others perceive me, and how my inner saboteur operates (followed by a synergistic discovery the novel ‘The Wind and the Willows’, a supposed children’s book which gives an insider perspective on social intelligence). I mentioned ‘Goat Foot God’ to you 2-3 weeks ago on a Magic Monday, and you suggested I read more of Dione Fortune’s novels, which I am now doing. I came across a helpful concept (I think from ‘The Secrets of Dr. Taverner’), in which Fortune states that in the normal course of life, a young person is outwardly focused, on such things as learning, a livelihood, bringing up a family and such, and that later in life, a person turns inward, towards spirituality or other inner disciplines. But if a child is brought up badly, then that normal sequence is upended, and the young person spends his life inwardly focused, and doesn’t attend properly to life’s external imperatives. (Oh man, that’s me to a ’T’, and I’ve long been so conscious of this inward focus, and its detriment to my daughters, that I revealed it to a psychologist; she was impressed by my insight, and she said “you’ve got to stop doing that!”. Yeah.) The next day I read the following in ‘Scripts People Live’ regarding childhood: “mother tells you what to do and father tells you how”. In an instant, a puzzle assembled itself. You might recall my comment in ‘A Useful Kind of Madness’, how I mentioned that my own father was one of those cranks of whom you spoke, and that my mother was autistic to the point she was unable to bond with me. Well, I now SEE how the dynamic I had with them as a small child works NOW. Bing.

My mind is yet again chewing on my single most recurrent, and clearest, memory: A nightmare I had at age 3. I won’t recount the details, except to say that it had two parts, the first in which I felt bathed in bliss in the knowledge of my Aunt’s love, and the second half in which I was plunged into horror and grief when I saw that my [now departed] mother did not love me, and that my Father seemed clueless. That horror portion was what my recollections would usually dwell upon, but the whole dream as always been at my fingertips. The meaning of this dream long eluded me. I only came to understand, at age 33, the horror portion. This insight came suddenly, as my Father lay on his deathbed, unable to speak or move. The meaning of the horror portion revealed itself in a shattering insight, showing how my father manifested his love for me. I was able to thank him, enabling him to depart to the next realm with some consolation.

It has taken another 30 years to figure out the meaning of the first, blissful, half of my dream. Just this week, I realized that the first part is more important that the second, and I think it is a road map. In that first part, I had felt swathed in my Aunt’s love even though we were apart(my Aunt lived far away, and I seldom saw her). Not only did I feel loved, but the dream landscape I surveyed was of an indescribable, intense beauty. Yet the landscape was mundane. What made it so indescribably beautiful was my perception of it, which was linked to my relationship with my Aunt. That memory is the ONLY memory I have of feeling loved when not in the presence of the person. The bliss I experienced is a human capacity. It doesn’t need to happen only once in a lifetime in a dream. I’m convinced it was the same experience Somerset Maugham, for example, describes at the end of ‘Of Human Bondage’.

What I have from my biological mother is the opposite of what my Aunt gave me, but that is what I live out of. My father, clueless nerd and crank that he was, gave me immersion lessons on how to be interpersonally inappropriate when not absent altogether (again from Scripts People Live: “mother tells you what to do and father tells you how”).

Now for my occult question:
JMG, surely you’ve heard of PTSD, and how people who suffer from this not only have unbidden recollections of their traumatic memories, but RELIVE them.

So, if people can relive traumatic memories, should it not be possible to relive sublime, blissful memories? That’s what I want to do: re-experience my dream memory of my Aunt’s love, so I can place that on my throne, and displace the Hellish emotion and self-image given by my mother.

*** So JMG, is there an occult process to relive a memory? What is it? ***

--Lunar Apprentice

replies.title: Re: Reliving memories
replies.content:
Lunar Apprentice, thank you for your profound and moving post. And thanks to JMG and other commenters for joining in.

"But if a child is brought up badly, then that normal sequence is upended, and the young person spends his life inwardly focused, and doesn’t attend properly to life’s external imperatives."

That relates to me as well. My mother was very sick much of my childhood. But because of whackadoodle beliefs indulged in by my parents, nobody was allowed to state this truth. In addition, she often had outbursts denouncing people, ideas, and situations, very inconsistently.

As a child, I tried to resolve the illogical paradoxes by concluding that I was shamefully bad and wrong, and that both I and the world were full of hidden danger of being Just Plain Wrong. Which meant I shouldn't do anything without being directed to do so. This has led to enormous needless loss, from my failing to move ahead when it actually was a safe, good idea for me to try something new.

I have come to believe four things about resolving the injuries of trauma. Perhaps they might help you or some other readers.

First is that the key to healing is that there is a consciously compassionate witness to the hurt, to the fact that the shocking and overwhelming event occurred, and that it was shocking and overwhelming for the one who experienced it.

Sometimes adults can do this for our own inner child. Sometimes, we need someone else to be present with us to provide the discovery that we can be loved and accepted, even while in the midst of pain and uncertainty.

Second is that healing may involve not just talking about the experience, but getting to complete physical motions that were blocked, prevented from completion. Somatic Experiencing and Primal Therapy are two such approaches.

Third, once there is the relief of tension, we readily create our own insights. We do not need someone else to explain to us what is a healthier way to think. Free of the burden of trying to grasp and control the present to prevent pain in the past, we readily, spontaneously see a better interpretation, a more empowering option.

Fourth, it's optional, but artwork and storytelling can help in this process. I've seen screenwriting instructors who say that one's first screenplay should deal with whatever are the biggest issues in one's own life, either literally or metaphorically showing what they are and how to properly resolve them.

This is true for me with writing my first novel. The kind, loving, supportive, empathetic, encouraging, helpful, close knit family was not just fun to envision and write about, but also cathartic for me to, in some ways, imagine what a happier childhood could have been like, even though there are also very substantial differences between the fictional family and my own.

Drawing, music, dancing, journaling by asking "and why do I feel that way?" then writing the answer, or simply envisioning a better past, and how that would have led to a happier present: these are also examples of such tools.

Mr. New-Writer

comments.title: On mutual reception and weak semi-squares
comments.content:
Following up from my question last week, I've had a ridiculous amount of fun looking into the historic frequency of mutual receptions. There's something redolent of stamp collecting about this so I was able to unleash my not very well hidden inner nerd. I looked at Aries, Cancer, Libra, Capricorn ingresses at London from 21st June 1800 17:51 UT to the present. The rule I used was that a for a given pair of planets, both were in the sign ruled by the other.

One slightly doubtful assumption to these figures is that a mutual reception involving the sun is sufficiently accidental to still be of interest.

It turns out that as far as I can tell there were 387 in the 220 year period. There were 60 examples of charts with two mutual receptions and 2 with a triple mutual reception. These last two where 23rd September 1974, at 09:58, which had Moon-Saturn, Mercury-Venus, and Jupiter-Neptune. The other was 22nd September 2004, 16:30 UT that had Sun-Venus, Moon-Saturn, and Uranus-Neptune.

Of interest also is Aries Ingress London 1939 which has two: Venus-Uranus, Mars-Saturn. Mutual receptions are absurdly laborious to spot without computer assistance - if you allow Pluto, there are 50 unique planetary pairs to check for mutual rulership. However the pairs happen frequently enough to consider if the rule implies one additional chart or three.

As for what it means beyond the obvious point that charts with one or more mutual receptions are even trickier to interpret than usual I currently remain clueless. Neither 1974 nor 2004 stand out as exceptional years compared with 2016-2021.

I have a complete list extending to 2032 if anyone wants a copy.

---

On the subject of whether or not to include the weak semi-squares in the 2020 inauguration chart, I made the following interpretation based on what I know about planetary personalities in mundane charts.

Sun-Neptune 48.6 (the president is slightly unpopular with the mass of non political people - little impact)
Moon-Neptune 40.1(the PMC is weakly unpopular with the mass of non political people - little impact)
Mars-Neptune 47.7 (non political people are in conflict? unsettled? disturbed? but not enough to matter)
Uranus-Neptune 47.8 (pivitol people or well known activists, perhaps Greta Thunberg types, are mildly unpopular with the mass of non-political people - little impact)

I assume little impact because the aspect is minor and because the examples are at the very edge of their orbs. They also seem very much par for the course except perhaps Uranus-Neptune. In general, PMs and presidents usually see dips in popularity after election, and the PMC/Aristocracy is never going to be popular. As for Uranus-Neptune, I should think that they swing from popular to unpopular depending on the times. They are only going to have an impact when they are at an extreme so a weak semi-square is of little interest I would have thought.

Are these on the right track or have I missed something?

Andy

comments.title: AWEN / AUM
comments.content:
I've been performing the Sphere of Protection now for that past several months, daily, with astoundingly better results than I ever got with the Lesser Banishing Ritual of the Pentagrams, or with the typical opening Pentagrams of protection ritual from Wiccan sources.
I also have tried calling on divine names from pantheons, but after failing to feel any sense of meaningful connection, I've gone to simply calling on the essential ideas of the divine and the elements, unnamed, which has also produced a much greater sense of connection with the powers. Maybe one day I will get a name come to me, but for now, I find myself just directly connecting to the currents and sense of the elements. It feels more basic and powerful. By the time I've finished my morning invocation, I feel alive, full of vim and vigour and ready to take on the day.
One of the elements that helps invigorate me is when I vibrate the sacred word AWEN. And I've felt that sense of vigour before, which leads me to wonder, where the word AWEN derived from?
Many, many moons ago, I read that the favourite "eastern" meditation word popularized in the New Age world "OOM" was actually supposed to be "AUM", pronounced so as to cause the chakras to resonate. That is, the tones begin as a deep "AAH" that one feels resonating in the pelvis chakra, followed by a modulation of pitch up to an "UUU" sound that moved the centre of vibration up through the charas in the bowels, stomach, chest, where it became an "OOO" resonating in the throat, and then to yet higher pitch until it became an "MMM" that was felt resonating in the third eye. Thus invigorating the chakras during meditation. (I actually met a yoga instructor from India who did not know that. Or that dogs actually stretch in the downward dog pose, hence the name. Go figure.)
As I vibrate the word AWEN it has almost exactly the same sequence and effect, beginning with a deep vibration in my pelvis and moving up my body to my forehead as the pitch changes.
I was wondering if this was pinched from Eastern sources and incorporated into the new Druid teachings some time in the past 250 years, or is it actually a co-incident and independent European spiritual development? I'd be tickled if it were the latter, but not at all surprised if the former.

Bruce
Renaissance Man

replies.title: Re: AWEN / AUM
replies.content: The way I was taught about it is that whenever there is a realized being the primal sound is there in one way or another. AUM, AMEN (pronounced correctly not as in English) and derivatives are meant to acknowledge that primal creative force. I don't know about AWEN but perhaps is similar.

replies.title: Re: AWEN / AUM
replies.content: The word awen is Welsh, and has been used since at least the early Middle Ages for the spirit of inspiration. (Giraldus Cambrensis mentions awenyddion, "people with awen," in his book The Description of Wales, which was written in 1194.) Iolo Morganwg wrote about it extensively in Barddas, and the Druid Revival got it from there. (An amusing story: I learned it from the OBOD correspondence course, which treats it as a sacred word to be chanted in public ritual; later, when I joined AODA, I received it as one of the inner secrets of a certain grade, and had to explain to the old guard that it wasn't exactly secret any more.) I suspect that it's a mix of your two possibilities; the word is ancient, but the habit of chanting it as a mantra was probably influenced by the Hindu and Buddhist use of AUM.

replies.title: Re: AWEN / AUM
replies.content:
I wonder (no big deal either way) if this is a case of two different cultures hitting on the same thing, or if they are both descended from some ancient knowledge that was known before the different Indo-European groups spread out and went their separate ways millennia ago?
At any rate, it certainly makes performing the SoP feel like I just had a pot of coffee.

Bruce
Renaissance Man

replies.content:

comments.title: Gravitational Anomalies: Tourist trap or something more?
comments.content:
Good morning JMG,

What is your take on "gravitational anomalies" that are commonly marketed as tourist attractions?

I ask, because up until about a week ago I would have said that they were most likely just wonky rooms full of optical illusions and hype. My family and I were up in the NC mountains last weekend, and it was raining, so we decided to do an indoor activity. There is one of these anomalies near Boone NC, which has been a tourist attraction for many years. I figured: "Why not go? It's a bit of local color, and at worst it'll be a fun story." To be sure, there was no shortage of schlock and kitsch, but the anomaly itself is another matter.

To confuse matters, the room *was* wonky. You reach the room from a small courtyard uphill from the anomaly. Going through the door, you enter a small hallway; you could bypass the anomaly room by going left, or enter the room by going straight down a ramp. (Note I will freely use "we" because the four of us discussed what we saw and felt, and were in agreement.) As we proceeded down the ramp, we began to feel an odd sensation. I could feel it most strongly in my head, possibly an inner ear effect: it felt somewhat like falling, and somewhat like being pulled forward (downhill). In my life I have walked and hiked on many slopes, some quite steep, and I've never felt like I was being pulled sideways. The room itself was about 10' wide across the hill, and 20' long up and down the hill. The upper part of the room was slightly sloped down, while the lower part was more sharply sloped down, and there were handrails all along the sides. In this room there was a pendulum (a broom hanging on a hook) with a protractor mounted (allegedly) level, a swing that appeared to be hanging at an odd angle, and a track on the other side wall that appeared to be sloping up steeply in the downhill direction. So with the "appears" and "allegedly" being assumed: The broom was hanging at a significant angle from level, I think about 25 degrees. Rubber balls, seemingly ordinary rubber handballs, would roll up the track, go into a hole, and reappear in a receptacle well below the starting point of the track. The swing hung at a downhill angle, and this was the equilibrium point whether or not someone was sitting or swinging on it. All the while we were in there, about ten, maybe fifteen minutes, we felt the disturbing sense of being off-balance; the handrails were very necessary! This feeling went away after moving only a short distance from the room, either back up the entry ramp, or into the next room.

Scientifically speaking, it felt as if there was a significant horizontal gravity vector. If I am remembering the 25 degree angle correctly, it would be a 0.47g horizontal acceleration, assuming an unchanged vertical acceleration of 1.0g, and assuming that the protractor was really level. The off-kilter nature of the room bothers me though. I wasn't expecting something real, so I didn't bring any instruments. Ideally, I'd put a laser level outside the anomaly and paint a true horizontal line on the wall inside the anomaly, and measure angles from that. Nevertheless, it is undeniable that the place felt seriously weird, and that the feeling was localized in that small space.

What are we dealing with here?

replies.title: Re: Gravitational Anomalies: Tourist trap or something more?
replies.content:
Replying to myself here... I can add that I have thought of possibilities - some occult, some not.

There is always the possibility that it is a really good illusion, but the distinct physical sensations (present even with eyes closed) associated with that one room suggest otherwise.

replies.title: Re: Gravitational Anomalies: Tourist trap or something more?
replies.content: It's not a gravitational anomaly but a look into the way your mind constructs the world of your experience out of sensory cues and memory. Those tourist traps are wonderful places to watch that process at work, because the sensory cues are set up to trick your mind into assembling them the wrong way -- to see things as level when they're not, for example, and see things as slanted one way when they're actually slanted a different way. I paid several visits to the Oregon Vortex in Gold Hill, OR, and had a fine time watching the way it was set up to mess with my head. It's definitely worth the price of admission!

replies.title: Re: Gravitational Anomalies: Tourist trap or something more?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The image of reality in your mind is not like a photograph taken of some scene. A photographis image simply captures parts of what is there according to the laws of optics. The image in your mind, however, is an out-and-out construct, rather like some artist's impressionistic painting of a dimly glimpsed landscape. This is because all your mind has to go on, as it constructs its image of reality, are the electro-chemical imputs given to your brain by your body's sense organs.

Yet the image you construct in your mind suffices for most practical purposes of your life. Also, since we humans are highly social animals, we always unconsciously check and cross-check our mental constructs against those of other people around us, so that all our constructed images of reality are roughly similar--similar enough for us all to get along in simple daily activities.

However, every person's mind always has the ability to create alternate, competing images of reality in the mind. Many of us are strongly encouraged to stop doing this in early childhood, that is, not to deviate from the consensus reality of people with whom we have formed social relations. Yet some of us have minds and nervous systems that won't entirely cooperate. In my own experience, many of these people are otherwise non-conformists to some degree. Might this be you, too?

Anyway, it's simply a mental glitch (characteristic especially of non-conformists), but (IMHO) it is an extremely valuablle and instructive glitch for anyone who is wondering what reality is really like, once one gets beyond all the words and allk the social pressures to conform.

One worth-while book on this is Peter L. Berger and Thomas Luckmann's The Social Construction of Reality (1966). In my own case, however, I learned about it from verious papers by the linguist, engineer and Theosophist, Benjamin Lee Whorf, colleced conveniently in his Language, Thought and Reality (Massachusetts Institute of Technology Press, 1956). Both of these can be found online at archive.org. (Of Whorf's many papers reprinted in that volume, the one that begins on page 134 is the most valuable foe what we're discussing, and the next best are the last four papers in the volume.)

I think I also have an anxiety response to those whose motivations are super self absorbed and narcissistic, like a physical warning sign, and the world appears different.  Thank you for the references, I will check them out.  Tamar

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
You're welcome, Tamar! Let me know, if you want to, what you get from the books I mentioned.

Berger & Luckmann's title can be taken in two ways: (1) our specific constructions of reality are achieved socially, or (2) reality is nothing but a construct of society. The first is what Berger and Luckmann are writing about. The second is what most readers obsessively want to argue against when they critique the book, and there is a whole academic industry on that theme. That's not what Berger anbd Luckmann were claiming.

Something similar happened almost from day one with Whorf's papers: most critics thoiught (and still think) he is saying that language wholly and deterministically creates their speakers' world views, and traps them as it does so. All Whorf was actually saying is that language has a mighty influence on how we habitually think and act; but his examples and other remarks can lead to profound insights. (The reader, as always, must do the hard work of understanding what the author has written.)

replies.title: Re: Celtic altar
replies.content:
The ancient Celts didn't use images of their gods. When the Celts under Brennus invaded Greece in 279 BC, the Celtic warriors laughed themselves into hiccups on seeing Greek statues of gods -- "They think their gods look like people! Har har har!" comments.title: experiment in computational occultism
comments.content:
So I had the idea the other day to combine a few ideas from computing and esotericism and experiment a bit:

- The idea that the brain is more like a radio than a computer.
- Radin's observations showing that people can influence the outcomes of random processes like radioactive decay.
- Sheldrake's ideas of patterns building up in informational space.

The experiment was to be set up as follows:

- Generate a random maze, and set up a recurrent neural network (a neural network that remembers some state from one run to another) that could choose directions to take in traversing the maze.
- Do not attempt to train the network using backpropagation, but assign parameters to the network from a true random source. There is a site random.org that apparently uses atmospheric noise to generate random numbers.
- Run the maze solver program repeatedly with new random parameters. See if maze-solving performance improves over time despite there being no explicit computational learning taking place.

The idea would be to see if some entity out there would be interested in using the program as a radio, in the same way that our minds use our brains. Alternatively maybe a pattern would become established in informational space a la Sheldrake.

But I've had second thoughts due to your description of demonic activity being characterized by repetitive mindless activity, so maybe repeately trying to traverse simple mazes would attract the wrong kind of attention.

I performed a shield chart geomantic divination with the question "Will the experiment I have been developing this week be safe for me and my family?" I got RW Caput Drac, LW Tristitia, J Conjunctio. In my very limited understanding this suggests to me that the idea has promise, in that it involves a combination of areas, but the particular experiment would be unsafe for me or my family.

So I have not performed the experiment.

I have some thoughts on maybe making it more safe. One might be to make the simulated environment for the neural nets to traverse be more interesting and dynamic, and thus attract a higher quality of tourist. Another would be to maybe derive my neural network parameters from radio from the Sun or Jupiter. I don't currently have the right hardware to pick up solar or Jovian radio, but I'm considering acquiring some.

What do you think?

replies.title: Re: Ley lines and what are they...?
replies.content:
Okay, you're going to hear a lot of handwaving and disagreement about this. My take is that leys (the proper term -- "ley lines" is repetitive) are an artifact. They were created in certain parts of the world many thousands of years ago by an ancient civilization. They certainly exist in Britain, Ireland, certain parts of northwestern continental Europe, and Japan, and there's some reason to think that some parts of New England and the Canadian Maritimes also have them, but they're not universal. Do they exist in North Carolina? You'd have to see if there are linear alignments of prehistoric mounds and other ceremonial locations -- that's how you tell.

Earth energies, by contrast, are everywhere. (The ley system was probably a vast project of sacred engineering used to work with earth energies.) Different places unquestionably have different energies, and some places have much stronger magical energies than others. (There's a reason, for example, why French occultists tend to gravitate toward Lyon -- that area has extremely strong magical energies.) So it's quite possible that Asheville is such a place. John Gilbert, one of my occult teachers, lived there for many years and thought highly of the local energies!

replies.title: Re: Avoiding False Jesii
replies.content: Prayer is like a radio, and the tuning dial is set by your own emotional and spiritual state. People who pray with a mind full of vanity and greed make contact with beings who correspond to that state of consciousness. People who pray with a mind full of faith and love make contact with beings who correspond to that state of consciousness. In practical terms, this is why it's a very good idea for Catholics to pray the rosary, concentrating on one or another set of mysteries at the appropriate intervals, and it's a good idea for anyone and everyone to read at least a short passage from sacred writings, or devotional or mystical literature, before praying.

replies.title: Re: Astral/Etheric Hygiene with Non-Monogamy
replies.content: All I can say is that everyone I know who tried to spice up their marriage or relationship with extramarital activity ended up having their marriage or relationship blow up on them within a year or so. I'm sure some people can manage it, but I've never met one.

replies.title: Re: Astral/Etheric Hygiene with Non-Monogamy
replies.content:
I know several people who've been successfully polyamorous for 15-20 years, all part of the same extended social circle, including couples in existing primary relationships that started over ten years ago. Without exception, all began their most enduring relationships as poly, and the lasting relationships are not with their first poly partner (in other words, they were already quite experienced in how poly worked for them and in their locality) - which I know is not great news for people considering opening up an existing monogamous relationship. There are probably examples of the latter being successful, but as JMG said about poly in general, I haven't encountered them.

Most of these people are great listeners and are very emotionally articulate - communication and empathy are essential, if not central, to poly. The same communication skills that make poly work can mean they make excellent friends too.

There may be people around you who are poly but about whom it's not obvious. Most of these friends look 'straighter' than the poly stereotypes and aren't necessarily out at work and so on.

But if this is the general model that people follow as they move through their reincarnation cycles, what do we make of these people who are supposed to embody all of the great attributes? Are we supposed to try to be philosopher priest kings in a single lifetime? Or do we slowly achieve this status over the culmination of many lifetimes without ever living up to the mutually conflicting goals embodied in the philosopher priest king during a single lifetime? Stated otherwise, should we try to be philosopher priest kings now, or should be choose one to focus on?

replies.title: Re: Philosopher Priest King
replies.content: Hermes Trismegistus, if he lived at all and was not simply a symbolic figure, was one of the greatest adepts who ever lived. He reached the ability to be prophet, priest and king all at once through many lives of sustained spiritual effort. Instead of trying to do that now, simply focus on whatever spiritual discipline works for you, and pursue it steadily while you go about the other aspects of your life and do your best. Once you achieve supreme adeptship, you can do what he did!

Hey JMG,

I just got a hold of your new edition of inside a magical lodge and it is a fantastic and fascinating book! I wanted to ask about the distinction between symbols and signs.

Symbols are invested with much more complex meaning inherent to what it is (the sun), whereas signs have meaning in an arbitrary fashion, depending upon what meaning the group ascribes to it (stop sign).

But how adaptable are signs and the meaning assigned? For example, could it be possible to come up with an AODA ritual using the hexagram as a representation of the seven elements, with spirit within in the center, similar to how the pentagram is used within five element conceptions? Obviously the symbolism assigned would be different from other uses of the hexagram in other orders. But the pentagram seems similarly adapted in the Druid magic handbook. However, if I’m understanding it correctly, the hexagram (or pentagram) itself is an adaptable sign capable of representing whatever symbolic correspondences are assigned to it, meaning that anyone could construct a hexagram ritual within their system if it uses thought out, symbolically intelligible, and balanced correspondences.

Am I understanding that correctly? Would there be any occult contraindications for these kinds of adaptations?

Thanks,
David

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Sure, you can come up with any sign you want to, and assign meanings to it as you like. If it's just a sign and not a symbol, though, its power will be limited, because it doesn't reach down into the deep places of being the way symbols do. We use established signs because those are generally also symbols.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: So in terms of the hexagrams — would you say that it is inextricably linked as a symbol to planetary correspondences?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: No, but you can't just up and assign anything to it at random. The sixfold symmetry, three poised against three, fits some things and not others.

comments.content:
Greetings JMG.

I was baptized Catholic at birth but about 10 years ago, for personal and familial reasons, I decided to leave for good and "tendered my resignation" by sending a formal request for debaptism to the Church. It was accepted, the local Bishop even sent me an official letter of excommunication and that was it. I did not take up any other spiritual practice and I just lived my life as a secular atheist for the next 10 years.

However the last 10 years haven't been kind to me: I fell into several addictions, ruined several relationships, stopped exercising, gained a lot of weight, jumped from dead end job to dead end job. Eventually I came around on the whole spirituality thing and started studying the occult and now I suspect that there might be a connection between my apostasy and my life's downward spiral.

Do you think it's possible that my excommunication might have distrupted a beneficial, protective relationship with a god (the Catholic Christian God and/or Jesus in this case), leaving me vulnerable to certain very noxious energies and nasty entities? Because in hindsight that's how I've always felt in the last 10 years, naked and vulnerable. As if unable to really control myself 100%. Only recently I've been able to gain some insight thanks to discursive meditation and journaling.

If that's the case, what can I do to patch things up? I'm very much a beginner in the occult, about six months of studying but no actual practice except as I said journaling and meditation.

Thank you very much for your time and your writings, they helped me immensely.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Anonymous,

I'm a Catholic who has recently taken up the occult. I can't give the kind of expert advice that JMG can but as far "patching things up," I can say that getting your excommunication lifted probably won't be very difficult. Your letter of resignation wouldn't have undone the effects of your baptism, which leaves an indelible mark on your soul. If you go to confession with a Catholic priest and tell him about this situation, he can either lift the excommunication (if the bishop has delegated that authority to him) or he can have you come back later (usually a few weeks), after which he'll have gotten the authority from the bishop. He can then finish the process by lifting your excommunication. That'd be the easiest way to patch things up with the Catholic God.

comments.title: Worship and Wine
comments.content:
Salutations JMG!

Materials are arriving in the mail, and I'm putting together the last bits and bobs needed for basic Neoplatonist polytheistic worship. I'm still reading through Ovid, but I had a few practical questions:

A libation of red wine and some frankincense is the daily recommended offering. I've read that after the offering is made it's eaten/drunk in silence as communion with the god it was offered to.

1) What if I hate the taste of most all alcohols? Do I still have to choke it down?

2) How much should I pour? I'd like to be a generous host, but I'd rather choke down only a shotglass of wine.

3) Do I pour a libation each time I sit down and drink it at the end of prayer? Or is one libation poured at the beginning of the day and emptied out after night time prayers? Or once a week? What's the proper schedule? I don't buy alcohol frequently, but I imagine it might get pretty expensive twice a day, and I'd feel guilty offering cheap box wine.

replies.title: Re: Worship and Wine
replies.content:
If you're pouring a libation, you don't drink it -- you pour it out on the ground. If you don't like drinking wine, you don't have to drink it, just offer it to the gods.

You can also do without the wine and simply burn some incense.

There is no fixed schedule, by the way.

replies.title: Re: Worship and Wine
replies.content: So for any offering made into an offering bowl on the altar (coins, wine, salt) how and when do I dispose of it?

replies.title: Re: Worship and Wine
replies.content: Depends on the offering. Wine can be poured out onto the earth at your convenience. Coins should be donated to charity as an offering. Salt probably needs to go into running water.

replies.title: Re: Worship and Wine
replies.content: Is it acceptable to bury or compost food items such as barley cakes or bread?

replies.title: Re: Worship and Wine
replies.content: Those were traditionally burnt. A barbecue does a good job of that.

replies.title: Re: Saltpeter
replies.content:
1) Trying to make memory images connect to each other in logical ways is very difficult, not least because you soon have more connections than images! Bruno advises linking the images together with connections, logical or symbolic, but you can also simply put things together in some very arbitrary way -- for example, everything in this room begins with the letter A.

2) I've worked with Lull's system, but it's not a standard art of memory, it's a conceptual algebra. If you want to explore it, this website is your go-to place for information on it.
